message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 16
Recent Green phase: added loyaltyDiscount (-20% on basePrice) when yearsWithMHPCO >= 2

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the Refactor phase following the disciplined process.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is â€” name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23â†’22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) â†’ `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” with the 3rd data point (staff 80â†’93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25â†’~33 â€” accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25â†’33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` â€” the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative â€” no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 Ã— 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 â†’ 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35â†’36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) â€” rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) â€” rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) â€” the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices Ã— 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~âˆ’9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" â€” rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list â†’ premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` â†’ `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative â€” next test ("4 runes â†’ no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function â€” rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) â€” three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~âˆ’3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change â€” the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it â€” confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) â†’ 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43	
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 Ã— 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals â†’ 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 Ã— 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured â€” making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones â†’ two separate blocks â†’ 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` â†’ `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) â€” rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` â€” both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function â€” rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- Change: reordered module-level constants by domain role. Now grouped as `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (with the new `moonstone: 25` entry added in Green) + `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` first (item-pricing data), followed by `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` + `PROCESSING_FEE` (premium-formula constants). Body of `quote` unchanged.
49	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the new ordering mirrors the function's computational flow (lookup base prices â†’ maybe apply block override â†’ apply surcharge â†’ add fee), making the data shape easier to scan as cycle 12 will introduce per-type block-pricing logic. Mass change: 0 (pure reorder). The "2 runes + 1 moonstone â†’ no block" test is the THIRD confirming test for the narrow `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard (after cycles 9 and 10): the `every((item) => item.type === "rune")` predicate correctly excludes the mixed-type case, so no structural change was needed. Cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones â†’ two separate blocks â†’ 120 G base") remains the genuine forcing test that will restructure the predicate into per-type block counting. Considered (and rejected) generalising the predicate now â€” would be triangulation on three confirming negatives. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a function or renaming it â€” rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, cycle-12 will reshape it). Considered deduplicating `rune: 25, moonstone: 25` via a shared constant â€” rejected as false coupling (the two types share a price by spec coincidence, not by definition). All existing names still fit.
50	- Naming established: none new; constant order is the only change.
51	
52	## After cycle 12
53	- Change: clarified the rune-component block-discount vocabulary. (1) Renamed `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` â†’ `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` â€” the cycle-12 Green generalised the rule to apply to both runes AND moonstones, so the "THREE_RUNES" name had become a lie. (2) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` to name the spec category "rune-class building components". (3) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` to name the domain literal (exactly 3 of a kind forms a block). (4) Extracted local `const formsRuneBlock = RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` to name the per-type-group predicate that the cycle-12 Green collapsed into the inline `if`.
54	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” after cycle 12, the inline guard `(type === "rune" || type === "moonstone") && count === 3` mixed three domain concepts (the rune-component type category, the block size, the block-formation predicate) as anonymous literals. Each now has a name that mirrors the spec language ("rune components", "block of three"). The renamed constant also resolves the stale "THREE_RUNES" name that no longer matched the moonstone case. Considered also generalising to `floor(count/blockSize)` blocks per type (would support a future "6 runes = 2 blocks at 120 G" test) â€” rejected as speculative triangulation; the current tests only pin down "exactly 3 of a kind". Considered renaming `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` to something more general like `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES` â€” rejected, "rune component" is the spec's noun phrase for runes+moonstones (per Example Mapping). Considered extracting a `priceForTypeGroup(type, count)` helper function â€” rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, locality preserved by inline ternary-ish branch). Mass change: ~+5 (3 new constant bindings + 1 local binding + 1 invocation `.has()` swapped in for `||`+`===`+`===` â€” net mass roughly neutral; accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent). All structural shape (countByType + per-type-group loop + branch) preserved.
55	- Naming established: `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` (Set replacing inline type union), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (replaces literal `3`), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` (renames `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`), `formsRuneBlock` (local per-type-group predicate); `quote`, `countByType`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE` unchanged.
56	
57	## After cycle 13
58	- No refactoring (justified): the "sword enchantment 4, not cursed â†’ 115 G" test passed immediately with zero implementation change â€” `enchantment` and `cursed` fields are not yet read, and the boundary-below-threshold case `4 < 5` correctly exercises the existing path (`sword: 100` base â†’ 115 G premium) unchanged. This is the FIRST confirming test for the upcoming high-enchantment-surcharge rule, NOT its forcing test. Cycle 14 ("enchantment exactly 5 â†’ +30 G") is the genuine forcing test for the high-enchantment surcharge; cycle 15 ("cursed â†’ +50 G") is the forcing test for the curse surcharge. Considered widening the `items` type from `{ type: string }[]` to `{ type: string; enchantment?: number; cursed?: boolean }[]` to honour what the test actually passes â€” rejected as premature; the type should grow when those fields are actually consumed (cycle 14), and adding optional fields now with no reader would be speculative. Considered pre-extracting a `surchargesFor(item)` helper returning 0 â€” rejected as pure YAGNI. Considered extracting a per-type-group `priceForGroup(type, count)` helper, collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge`/`premium`/`items` locals, or renaming `quote` â€” all rejected on prior-cycle precedent (cycles 4, 6, 8, 10: test vocabulary earns each value its own name; locality preserved when used once; "quote" is the domain action). All existing names still fit. Mass unchanged.
59	
60	## After cycle 14
61	- Change: three coordinated improvements after the high-enchantment-surcharge forcing test (sword ench 5 â†’ 145 G) went green. (1) Renamed misleading local `itemPremium` (which held `basePrice + per-item enchantment surcharges` â€” never a "premium") into a separate `highEnchantmentSurcharges` accumulator initialised to 0; the per-item loop now adds only the surcharge contribution. (2) Made the premium assembly explicit and ordered to mirror R10: `const premium = basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE`. (3) Extracted module-level `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` (grouped above the existing policy-formula constants), removing two magic numbers (`5`, `0.3`).
62	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the post-Green `itemPremium` name was a lie (it was not a premium, just a mid-pipeline subtotal that conflated base price with one specific item-level surcharge). The new shape names each surcharge family separately, and the explicit four-summand `premium` line reads as R10's stacking order verbatim ("item base + item-specific surcharges + first-insurance surcharge + processing fee"). The constants match R5's exact spec phrasing ("enchantment level â‰¥ 5", "30% surcharge"). Setup benefit for cycle 16 (cursed surcharge): it will drop in as a parallel `cursedSurcharges` accumulator in the same per-item loop, slotted into the same `premium` sum, with no restructuring. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + high-ench) into one pass for Rule 3 (DRY) â€” rejected: the block-discount step needs `countByType` fully built before computing `basePrice`, and only then can per-item-base lookups be meaningful; the two loops solve different sub-problems despite looping over the same collection. Considered keeping `itemPremium` as a rolling subtotal and only adding a comment â€” rejected: misleading names are a smell that comments cannot fix. Considered NOT naming `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`/`_RATE` yet ("one data point") â€” rejected: cycle 13 (boundary 4) plus cycle 14 (exactly 5) together pin down both the threshold and the rate as named domain concepts, and the next two todos (cycles 15-16) reuse these exact values in test names ("enchantment 5", "30 high-ench"). Mass change: ~+5 (two new constants, one extra `+` in the premium line, one new binding `highEnchantmentSurcharges`; offset by literalsâ†’named-constants which keep mass weight the same). Accepted on cycle-4/6/8/12 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the values.
63	- Naming established: `highEnchantmentSurcharges` (local per-item-surcharge accumulator, replaces misleading `itemPremium`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.3`); `quote`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `countByType`, and all R3/R7/R9 constants unchanged.
64	
65	## After cycle 15
66	- Change: extracted module-level `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (grouped with the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` constants above the policy-formula block), replacing the literal `0.5` in the per-item cursed surcharge accumulator. The cycle-15 Green added `cursedSurcharges` as a parallel accumulator to `highEnchantmentSurcharges`, but with the surcharge rate as a magic literal `0.5` â€” asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` extracted in cycle 14.
67	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the literal `0.5` is the domain "curse surcharge rate" per spec E6 ("cursed â†’ +50% of base price"). The test name reads "100 + 50 curse + 30 high-ench"; both surcharges appear side-by-side in the premium and now both rates are named side-by-side at module scope, so the two surcharge lines read identically (`base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` vs. `base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the structural duplication between the two surcharge rules was visually obscured by one being named and the other being a literal; symmetric naming makes the parallel explicit and sets up cycle-16+ adding loyalty/follow-up rates in the same constant block. Mass change: ~+2 (one new binding + one new constant at module scope, offset by literal â†’ named-binding swap in the loop). Accepted on cycle-14 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered merging the two surcharge `if`s into a rules array iteration â€” rejected as speculative and would obscure per-policy clarity. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + per-item-surcharges) â€” rejected on cycle-14 precedent: the two loops solve different sub-problems (group-counting for block discount vs. per-item-instance surcharges) and the basePrice must be assembled before per-item lookups are meaningful in the eventual loyalty-discount path. Considered renaming `cursedSurcharges` â†’ `curseSurcharges` for consistency with `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` â€” kept `cursedSurcharges` since "cursed" is the spec adjective on items and the plural reads as "surcharges from cursed items". `quote` name and all other names still fit.
68	- Naming established: `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`); `cursedSurcharges` accumulator (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.
69

content.text:
Now I'll evaluate refactoring opportunities.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

Looking at the cycle-16 Green code, the literal `0.2` is the magic number that mirrors the cycle-14/15 pattern. Both `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` are named module constants, but the loyalty discount rate is an inline literal â€” asymmetric with the other policy adjustments.

The `loyaltyDiscount` local binding name is good (mirrors the test name "loyalty discount applies"). The threshold `>= 2` (years) is also a domain-named magic number per spec.

**Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (cycle-16 Green code)**

Focus on the newly added line:
```typescript
const loyaltyDiscount = input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? basePrice * 0.2 : 0;
```
Constants: `2`, `0.2`, `0` = 3
Bindings: `loyaltyDiscount`, `input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO`, `basePrice` = 3
Invocations: `>=`, `*` = 2 invocations Ã— 2 = 4
Conditional: `? :` = 4
Total for this line: 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 14

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 16 tests passing.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Two magic numbers (`2`, `0.2`) that have spec-given names ("2 years", "20% discount"). The cycle-14/15 precedent is to extract these as named constants to mirror spec vocabulary. Also matches the symmetric style of `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): None directly added; but the loyalty pattern parallels the customer-policy-rule structure.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Adding constants increases element count, but Rule 2 > APP.

The naming refactor is clearly indicated: extract `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` to match the established style. This also sets up cycle 17 ("1 year boundary") and cycle 18 ("3-year first-quote") which will reuse these names.

**Step 4: Implement Refactoring**

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-58-45_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context â€” no need to Read it back)

input.new_string:
const qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount = input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS;
  const loyaltyDiscount = qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount ? basePrice * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;

content.text:
All 16 tests still pass.

**Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass**

```typescript
const qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount = input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS;
const loyaltyDiscount = qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount ? basePrice * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
```
Line 1: bindings (`qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount`, `input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS`) = 3, invocation `>=` = 2; subtotal 5
Line 2: bindings (`loyaltyDiscount`, `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount`, `basePrice`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`) = 4, invocation `*` = 2, conditional `? :` = 4, constant `0` = 1; subtotal 11
Total: 16

Plus 2 new module constants (mass 1 each = 2), removing 2 literals from the body (-2). Net mass change: ~+2 to +4. Accepted on cycle-14/15 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value).

**Step 6: Document Decision**

Refactoring applied:
- Extracted `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` to mirror the established cycle-14/15 naming pattern (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`/`_RATE`, `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`).
- Extracted local `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount` predicate so the discount line reads as "if-qualifies â†’ apply-rate" instead of mixing the eligibility check with the rate arithmetic.
- This sets up cycle 17 (boundary "1 year â†’ no loyalty") to read `< LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` naturally, and cycle 20 (follow-up discount) to add a parallel `FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE` constant in the same block.

**Step 7: Append to Architecture Notes**

input.old_string:
## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted module-level `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (grouped with the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` constants above the policy-formula block), replacing the literal `0.5` in the per-item cursed surcharge accumulator. The cycle-15 Green added `cursedSurcharges` as a parallel accumulator to `highEnchantmentSurcharges`, but with the surcharge rate as a magic literal `0.5` â€” asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` extracted in cycle 14.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the literal `0.5` is the domain "curse surcharge rate" per spec E6 ("cursed â†’ +50% of base price"). The test name reads "100 + 50 curse + 30 high-ench"; both surcharges appear side-by-side in the premium and now both rates are named side-by-side at module scope, so the two surcharge lines read identically (`base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` vs. `base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the structural duplication between the two surcharge rules was visually obscured by one being named and the other being a literal; symmetric naming makes the parallel explicit and sets up cycle-16+ adding loyalty/follow-up rates in the same constant block. Mass change: ~+2 (one new binding + one new constant at module scope, offset by literal â†’ named-binding swap in the loop). Accepted on cycle-14 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered merging the two surcharge `if`s into a rules array iteration â€” rejected as speculative and would obscure per-policy clarity. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + per-item-surcharges) â€” rejected on cycle-14 precedent: the two loops solve different sub-problems (group-counting for block discount vs. per-item-instance surcharges) and the basePrice must be assembled before per-item lookups are meaningful in the eventual loyalty-discount path. Considered renaming `cursedSurcharges` â†’ `curseSurcharges` for consistency with `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` â€” kept `cursedSurcharges` since "cursed" is the spec adjective on items and the plural reads as "surcharges from cursed items". `quote` name and all other names still fit.
- Naming established: `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`); `cursedSurcharges` accumulator (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted module-level `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (grouped with the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` constants above the policy-formula block), replacing the literal `0.5` in the per-item cursed surcharge accumulator. The cycle-15 Green added `cursedSurcharges` as a parallel accumulator to `highEnchantmentSurcharges`, but with the surcharge rate as a magic literal `0.5` â€” asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` extracted in cycle 14.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the literal `0.5` is the domain "curse surcharge rate" per spec E6 ("cursed â†’ +50% of base price"). The test name reads "100 + 50 curse + 30 high-ench"; both surcharges appear side-by-side in the premium and now both rates are named side-by-side at module scope, so the two surcharge lines read identically (`base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` vs. `base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the structural duplication between the two surcharge rules was visually obscured by one being named and the other being a literal; symmetric naming makes the parallel explicit and sets up cycle-16+ adding loyalty/follow-up rates in the same constant block. Mass change: ~+2 (one new binding + one new constant at module scope, offset by literal â†’ named-binding swap in the loop). Accepted on cycle-14 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered merging the two surcharge `if`s into a rules array iteration â€” rejected as speculative and would obscure per-policy clarity. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + per-item-surcharges) â€” rejected on cycle-14 precedent: the two loops solve different sub-problems (group-counting for block discount vs. per-item-instance surcharges) and the basePrice must be assembled before per-item lookups are meaningful in the eventual loyalty-discount path. Considered renaming `cursedSurcharges` â†’ `curseSurcharges` for consistency with `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` â€” kept `cursedSurcharges` since "cursed" is the spec adjective on items and the plural reads as "surcharges from cursed items". `quote` name and all other names still fit.
- Naming established: `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`); `cursedSurcharges` accumulator (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.

## After cycle 16
- Change: extracted two module-level constants `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` (grouped in a new customer-policy block between the item-surcharge rates and the premium-formula constants), replacing the two magic literals `2` and `0.2` in the cycle-16 Green's inline ternary. Also split the ternary into two lines: `const qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount = input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS;` and `const loyaltyDiscount = qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount ? basePrice * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;`. The `premium` assembly line (subtracting `loyaltyDiscount`) and the rest of the module unchanged.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) â€” the cycle-16 Green left `2` (years threshold per spec C2) and `0.2` (20% discount rate per spec C2) as bare literals, asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` (cycle 14) and `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (cycle 15). The test name uses "loyalty discount" and the spec uses "2 years" / "20%" verbatim; both values earn names. Splitting `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount` out of the ternary names the eligibility check as a separate domain concept (cycle 17, the boundary test "1 year â†’ no loyalty", will read `< LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` naturally; cycle 20's follow-up discount will likely add `FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE` in the same constant block and possibly mirror the predicate). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the implicit parallel between the loyalty discount and the upcoming follow-up discount (cycle 20: `âˆ’15 follow-up`) is now visually anchored to a named customer-policy section. Mass change: ~+4 (two new module constants + one new local binding, offset by two literal â†’ named-constant swaps and ternary line-split). Accepted on cycle-14/15 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered keeping the single-line ternary for Rule 4 (fewest elements) â€” rejected: cycle 17's boundary test and cycle 18's "3 yrs first quote" both reuse the eligibility predicate semantically; naming it now prevents an awkward inline `(input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS)` repeated in the follow-up code path. Considered renaming `quote` to `computePremium` now that we have a customer-policy step â€” rejected, "quote" remains the spec verb and the function still returns the quoted premium. Considered extracting a `policyAdjustmentsFor(customer, basePrice)` helper that returns the net loyalty/follow-up delta â€” rejected as speculative; follow-up discount only arrives in cycle 20, and the current inline form keeps locality. Considered also renaming `loyaltyDiscount` â†’ `loyaltyDiscountAmount` to disambiguate from the rate â€” rejected: "discount" already reads as an amount in the `premium` subtraction line, and the cycle-15 `cursedSurcharges` precedent keeps amounts as plain nouns and rates as `*_RATE`.
- Naming established: `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` (module-private constant, replaces literal `2`), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.2`), `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount` (local predicate binding); `loyaltyDiscount` (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.

content.text:
Task #1 is in progress and remains so (cycle 16 of 21 quote tests complete). No state change needed.

---

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring**: Extracted two module-level constants `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, plus a local predicate binding `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount`, to remove the two magic literals from the cycle-16 Green's inline ternary and mirror the established cycle-14/15 customer/item-policy constant naming pattern.

**Simple Design Rules applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Spec vocabulary "2 years" and "20% discount" now named at module scope, symmetric with `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD/_RATE` and `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Eligibility predicate named, ready for cycle 17 boundary test and cycle 20 follow-up parallel

**Mass Change**: ~+4 (two new constants + one new binding, partially offset by literalâ†’named-constant swaps). Accepted on cycle-14/15 precedent (Rule 2 > APP).

**Tests**: 16 passing (22 todo).
